mnemonics.title: ONTO Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: ONTO = Every Output Now To be Opened (every output/codomain element must be opened/mapped to)

mnemonics.title: Surjective Reminder
mnemonics.content: SURJECTIVE: S-U-R-J-E-C-T-I-V-E - 'Somebody Used Red Juice Every Time, Insisting Everyone's Valid' - Every codomain element gets mapped!

mnemonics.title: Onto vs Into
mnemonics.content: ONTO = All covered (codomain = range), INTO = Some left out (range ⊂ codomain)

acronyms.usage: Used in formula m! × S(n,m) to count onto functions from n-element set to m-element set

acronyms.expansion: Function f from set A (domain) to set B (codomain)
acronyms.usage: Standard notation for defining functions between sets

visualTips.visualization: Draw two circles - left is domain (input set), right is codomain (output set). Draw arrows from each domain element to codomain elements. For onto, every codomain element must have at least one incoming arrow.
Draw mapping diagram. Onto: no 'lonely' elements in codomain (every right-side element has ≥1 arrow pointing to it)
Imagine codomain elements as 'pigeonholes' and domain elements as 'pigeons'. For onto, all holes must have at least one pigeon.

analogies.analogy: Like a door-to-door salesman who visits EVERY house in a neighborhood (codomain) at least once. Some houses may be visited multiple times, but none are missed.
Codomain is like the list of possible grades (A,B,C,D,E,F) a student can get. Range is the actual grades earned. Onto means all possible grades were actually awarded to some student.

If every student in class A passes to class B, and every student in class B graduates, then every student in class A eventually graduates. The composition of 'passing' and 'graduating' operations is 'graduating'.
